description: Jollof rice and beans cooked together in one pot — the beans parboiled first, then merged into the tomato base with the rice. Bold, filling, and deeply flavoured.

ingredients.name: parboiled long grain rice
ingredients.note: Wash until water runs clear. Parboiled only — regular rice will go mushy before beans finish cooking.

ingredients.name: honey beans (oloyin)
ingredients.note: Must be parboiled and partially cooked before going into the jollof. Brown beans or black-eyed peas work too.

ingredients.name: red bell pepper (tatashe)
ingredients.note: Essential for the deep red colour and body of the jollof base.

ingredients.name: scotch bonnet pepper (ata rodo)
ingredients.note: Adjust to your heat tolerance. The beans absorb heat well so do not over-pepper.

ingredients.name: onion
ingredients.note: 1 for parboiling beans, 1 into the blend, 1 sliced for frying the base.

ingredients.name: vegetable oil
ingredients.note: Neutral oil for frying the base.

ingredients.name: palm oil
ingredients.note: Optional addition to deepen the beans flavour within the jollof. Stir in with the beans.

ingredients.name: chicken stock
ingredients.note: Or beef stock. Cube dissolved in water is acceptable.

ingredients.name: ground crayfish
ingredients.note: Bridges the beans and jollof flavours beautifully. Highly recommended.

ingredients.name: curry powder
ingredients.note: Nigerian curry powder for the authentic jollof base flavour.

ingredients.name: bay leaves
ingredients.note: Add with the stock. Remove before serving.

ingredients.name: white pepper
ingredients.note: Subtle warmth. Black pepper substitutes fine.

ingredients.name: salt
ingredients.note: Season carefully — beans absorbs salt slowly so taste after every addition.

ingredients.name: foil or heavy lid
ingredients.note: For the steam-finish. Foil under lid creates the smoky base.

steps.title: Parboil the Beans
steps.instruction: The beans need a head start before anything else — they take significantly longer to cook than rice and the tomato acid in the jollof base will slow their softening further, so getting them 70% done before they join the pot is not optional. Sort through the beans and remove any stones or shrivelled ones, then rinse thoroughly. Place in a pot, cover with plenty of water, bring to a hard boil and boil for 15 minutes — this first cook removes gas-causing compounds. Drain and discard this first water completely. Return the beans to the pot with fresh water and one roughly chopped onion and cook on medium-high until the beans are about 70% done — soft on the outside with a slightly firm but not chalky centre. Drain and set aside.
micro_steps: Sort through 1½ cups of honey beans and remove any stones or shrivelled beans
Rinse under cold running water three times until the water runs fairly clear
Place in a pot and cover with plenty of water — at least 5cm above the beans
Bring to a hard rolling boil over high heat and boil for 15 minutes
Drain and discard all this first water completely
Return beans to the pot with fresh water to cover, add 1 roughly chopped onion
Cook on medium-high heat for 25–35 minutes
At 25 minutes, press one bean firmly between thumb and forefinger
It should feel soft on the outside with a slight resistance in the centre — not chalky, not fully creamy
If still too firm, continue cooking and test every 5 minutes
Drain the beans and set aside — do not rinse
prep_notes: Start the beans first — they take the longest and need to be done before you begin the base
Blend the tomato base (tomatoes, tatashe, scotch bonnet, 1 onion) while the beans are on their second cook
Wash the rice until the water runs clear and leave to drain in a colander
Have the chicken stock measured and ready — it goes in at a specific moment and you do not want to be measuring mid-cook
steps.sensory_cue: At 70% done, a single bean pressed between your fingers should feel uniformly soft on the outside surface but give just a little resistance when pressed firmly — not chalky or crunchy, but not fully smooth and creamy either. The cooking liquid will be slightly cloudy and earthy-smelling, which is correct.
steps.failure_risk: Under-cooked beans entering the jollof pot will stay stubbornly firm — the tomato acid in the base actively slows bean softening and there is no way to speed it up from inside the pot. Getting to 70% before merging is the only path to fully cooked beans in the finished dish. Over-cooked beans that are already fully soft will dissolve completely into the jollof and disappear.

steps.title: Blend the Tomato Base
steps.instruction: Place the plum tomatoes, red bell pepper, scotch bonnet, and 1 roughly chopped onion into a blender. Do not add water. Blend until completely smooth — no visible chunks or pieces. The blend should be a thick, vibrant, deep red-orange liquid that coats a spoon. Slice the remaining onion into rings and set aside for frying.
micro_steps: Quarter the plum tomatoes and add to the blender
Add the red bell pepper, roughly chopped
Add the scotch bonnet and 1 roughly chopped onion
Do not add water
Blend on high until completely smooth — about 30 seconds
Check: there should be no visible chunks of tomato skin or pepper
If the blend looks very thin and watery, pour into a bowl and set aside — it will fry down
Slice the remaining onion into rings and set aside
steps.sensory_cue: The blended base should be smooth, deep red-orange, and thick enough to coat the back of a spoon. It should smell fresh and sharp — the raw tomato and pepper smell is strong and correct at this stage. If it smells or looks very watery, the tomatoes had high water content — that is fine, just plan to fry the base for slightly longer.
steps.failure_risk: A watery blend that has not been sufficiently fried down produces a sour, thin jollof base that cannot carry both rice and beans properly. If the blend looks unusually thin, increase the frying time in the next step until the oil clearly rises to the surface regardless of how long it takes.

steps.title: Fry the Base
steps.instruction: Heat the vegetable oil in a large, heavy-bottomed pot over medium-high heat. Add the sliced onion rings and fry, stirring occasionally, for 3–4 minutes until soft and golden. Add the tomato paste and fry vigorously for 3 minutes, stirring constantly, until it darkens from bright orange to a deep brick colour. Pour in the blended tomato mixture, increase to high heat, and fry the base — stirring every 90 seconds — until it is very thick, deeply coloured, and the oil has risen clearly to the surface. This is a longer fry than for plain jollof because this base needs to be concentrated enough to carry both rice and beans.
micro_steps: Heat ⅓ cup of vegetable oil in your largest, heaviest pot over medium-high heat
Add the sliced onion rings and stir immediately
Fry for 3–4 minutes until the onion is soft and golden
Add 2 tablespoons of tomato paste and stir vigorously
Fry the paste for 3 minutes, stirring constantly, until it deepens to dark brick-red
Pour in all the blended tomato, tatashe, and scotch bonnet mixture
Increase heat to high and stir everything together
Fry at high heat, stirring every 90 seconds, for 20–25 minutes
The base is ready when it is thick and paste-like, dark brick-red, and oil is clearly floating on the surface
Any raw tomato smell must be completely gone before proceeding
steps.sensory_cue: The fully fried base should look very thick and almost paste-like — dark brick-red with oil clearly floating on the surface. Dragging a spoon through it should leave a trail that closes slowly. The raw tomato smell must be completely replaced by a rich, deep, fried-tomato aroma. If you can still smell raw tomato sharpness, the base is not ready.
steps.failure_risk: Insufficient frying leaves a raw, sour tomato flavour running through the entire finished dish that neither the rice nor the beans can absorb away. The oil rising clearly to the surface is your reliable, non-negotiable signal that the base is done — not the clock. Give it the full time it needs.

steps.title: Season the Base
steps.instruction: Reduce the heat to medium. Add the curry powder, thyme, white pepper, and ground crayfish, stirring each one in thoroughly. Crush the seasoning cubes to powder and add. If using palm oil, add it now and stir in — it will melt into the base and deepen both the colour and the beans flavour that comes later. Taste and add salt carefully. The base should taste bold, rich, and slightly over-seasoned at this point — it needs to be, because both the rice and beans will absorb seasoning and dilute it significantly.
micro_steps: Reduce heat to medium
Add 1½ tsp curry powder and stir in
Add 1 tsp thyme and stir in
Add ½ tsp white pepper and stir in
Add 1½ tablespoons of ground crayfish if using — stir in thoroughly
Crush 2 seasoning cubes to powder and add — stir until dissolved
Add 2 tablespoons of palm oil if using and stir until fully incorporated
Taste the base — it should be bold and intense
Add salt in small pinches, tasting between each addition
The base should taste slightly more seasoned than you want the finished dish — this is correct
steps.sensory_cue: The seasoned base should smell layered and complex — curry, thyme, and crayfish all distinct and present. The colour should be a warm, deep orange-red. On the palate it should taste bold and rich — perhaps slightly more intense than feels comfortable, but that intensity is exactly what you need before the rice and beans go in and absorb it.
steps.failure_risk: Timid seasoning at this stage is the most common jollof rice and beans failure — the vast volume of rice and beans about to go in will absorb and dilute whatever is in the base. Season boldly and confidently here. This is your last real opportunity to control the final flavour of the dish.

steps.title: Add Stock and Beans
steps.instruction: Pour in the chicken stock and stir to combine it with the seasoned base. Add the bay leaves. Bring the pot to a boil over medium-high heat, stirring to lift any base from the bottom. Once boiling, add the parboiled beans and stir gently to distribute them evenly throughout the liquid — do not mash or press them. Taste the liquid one more time: it should be rich, well-seasoned, and slightly bold. Let everything return to a full boil before the rice goes in.
micro_steps: Pour 2 cups of chicken stock into the pot and stir to combine with the base
Add 2 bay leaves and stir in
Increase heat to medium-high and bring to a boil — stir occasionally to lift base off the bottom
Once boiling, add the drained parboiled beans
Stir gently to distribute the beans evenly — use a folding motion, not vigorous stirring
Taste the liquid — it should be rich, savoury, and well-seasoned
Adjust salt if needed — add in small pinches only
Let the pot return to a full, active boil before the next step
steps.sensory_cue: The pot should be bubbling actively with the beans suspended and distributed throughout the deep red broth. The smell should be full, rich, and deeply savoury — the combination of the jollof base and beans stock creating something more complex than either alone. The liquid should look thick and deeply coloured, not thin or pale.
steps.failure_risk: Adding the rice to a pot where the beans are not evenly distributed results in pockets of beans in some areas and none in others — the final dish will be uneven. Take the extra few seconds to stir the beans through properly and confirm the liquid is at a full boil before adding the rice, so cooking starts evenly from the moment the rice goes in.

steps.title: Add Rice and Set Heat
steps.instruction: Add the washed, drained parboiled rice to the boiling pot. Stir well immediately so every grain is coated in the red base and the beans are evenly distributed throughout. Check the liquid level — it should come just to the surface of the rice and beans mixture, or about 1cm above. If it falls short, add a small splash of hot stock or hot water. If there is too much liquid, let it boil uncovered for 2 minutes to reduce before sealing. Reduce heat to the absolute lowest setting on your stove.
micro_steps: Add the washed, drained rice to the boiling pot
Stir immediately and thoroughly so every grain is coated in the red base
Ensure the beans are evenly distributed throughout the rice — no clumps
Check the liquid level — it should just reach the surface of the rice mixture
If liquid is below the surface, add a splash of hot stock or hot water
If liquid is more than 1cm above the surface, boil uncovered for 2 minutes to reduce
Reduce heat to the absolute lowest setting
Every grain should look stained deep orange-red at this point
steps.sensory_cue: Every grain of rice should be visibly stained orange-red from the moment it is stirred into the base — pale or white grains mean they are not coated. The beans should be evenly visible throughout, not clustered. The liquid should be sitting at or just barely above the surface of the rice mixture — not pooling above it, not absent below it.
steps.failure_risk: Too much liquid produces wet, mushy, overcooked rice and disintegrated beans — the dish loses all texture. Too little and the rice will not cook through before the base burns on the bottom. Getting the liquid level right at this stage is the most precise part of the recipe — take an extra 30 seconds to check it carefully before sealing the pot.

steps.title: Seal and Steam — First Cook
steps.instruction: Tear a sheet of foil large enough to cover the entire opening of the pot. Place it directly on the surface of the rice and beans mixture, pressing it gently to the edges. Put the lid on tightly over the foil to create a complete seal. Cook on the lowest heat possible for 25 minutes without opening the lid at any point. The steam trapped inside is simultaneously cooking the rice and finishing the beans. A faint smoky aroma drifting from the bottom of the pot after 15–20 minutes is the signature of Nigerian jollof and is expected.
micro_steps: Tear a sheet of foil large enough to cover the full interior opening of the pot
Place the foil directly onto the surface of the rice and beans — press gently to the edges
Place the lid firmly on top of the foil
Ensure the seal is as tight as possible — press the lid down
Reduce heat to the absolute lowest setting on your stove
Set a timer for 25 minutes
Do not open the lid at any point during these 25 minutes
After 15–20 minutes you may notice a faint smoky smell — this is correct and desirable
If the smoky smell becomes sharp or acrid at any point, the heat is too high — reduce it immediately
steps.sensory_cue: Steam should be escaping gently from the edges of the lid — a thin, steady wisp, not violent puffing. A gentle, faintly smoky aroma from the bottom of the pot after 15–20 minutes is the signature jollof smell and signals that a light caramelised crust is forming on the very bottom layer — this is the most prized part of Nigerian jollof.
steps.failure_risk: Opening the lid during the 25-minute steam cook releases the trapped steam that is doing the cooking — you lose heat and moisture simultaneously and the rice will cook unevenly, leaving some grains hard and others overcooked. Stay completely out of it for the full 25 minutes. If the smoky smell turns sharp or burnt, reduce the heat and do not open — just lower the temperature.

steps.title: Check, Fold and Finish
steps.instruction: After 25 minutes, open the lid carefully — stand back as steam will escape. Press a grain of rice between your fingers and a bean between yours — both should be completely soft with no resistance. If anything is still firm, add 2 tablespoons of hot water, re-seal tightly, and give it 10 more minutes before checking again. If everything is fully cooked, use a large wooden spoon to fold gently from the bottom once — one slow, deliberate turn that brings the bottom layer to the top. Remove and discard the bay leaves. Re-seal and cook on the lowest heat for a final 10 minutes. Rest uncovered for 5 minutes before serving.
micro_steps: Open the lid carefully — stand back from the steam
Remove the foil carefully — it will be very hot
Press a grain of rice firmly between your fingers — it should crush completely with no resistance
Press a bean between your fingers — it should be fully soft and creamy throughout
If either still has resistance, add 2 tablespoons of hot water, re-seal, and cook 10 more minutes
If both are fully cooked, use a large wooden spoon to fold once from the bottom — one slow, gentle turn
Find and remove both bay leaves
Replace the foil and lid to re-seal
Cook on the lowest heat for a final 10 minutes
Turn off heat and rest uncovered for 5 minutes before serving
steps.sensory_cue: Finished jollof rice and beans should be fully cooked, with each grain of rice and each bean separate and identifiable — nothing dissolved or mushy. The colour should be a deep, rich orange-red throughout. The aroma should be deeply savoury, fragrant, and slightly smoky. The very bottom layer will have a lightly caramelised, slightly crispy crust — this is the most coveted part of the pot.
steps.failure_risk: Folding too vigorously or more than once breaks the rice grains and crushes the beans into a mash — you lose the distinct textures that make this dish satisfying. One slow, gentle fold only. If the rice is still underdone at the check stage, always add water and re-seal rather than stirring aggressively or increasing the heat — both will make things worse, not better.
